card.title: A platform built for slides that needed to learn how to show documents.
card.subtitle: A reader redesign to make SlideShare's 30M+ slides and documents actually readable.

sections.title: A reader built for slides but full of documents nobody could read.
sections.subtitle: SlideShare is a global UGC platform with 70M+ users, originally built around slide presentations. I redesigned the document reader to improve readability across multiple formats — increasing engagement and ad revenue.

meta.team: Product Designer · UX Researcher · Product Manager · Lead Engineer

sections.label: THE PROBLEM
sections.title: SlideShare's reader wasn't built for reading documents. At all.
sections.body: Users needed a reader that got out of the way: one that let them evaluate documents quickly and read without distraction. For the product, longer and more focused sessions meant more ad value and a layout that could finally scale across formats.

sections.label: THE APPROACH
sections.title: Expanding the reading model — not just the layout.
sections.body: I redefined the visual hierarchy to support multiple formats and align with Scribd's design system. I simplified the surrounding interface and rebalanced ads and recommendations — keeping the document central without compromising revenue.

image.alt: SlideShare redesigned reader on a laptop

sections.label: MEASURING IMPACT
sections.title: The numbers moved in the right direction.
sections.body: Users stayed longer after removing friction. Mobile-first focused sessions on smaller screens. Users went deeper into documents. Users stayed long enough to read offline.

cards.label: Recommendations, metadata, ads, and actions competed for space.
The reader had no way to adapt to different formats.
Competing elements and a rigid model made reading complex.

sections.title: How do you navigate between what users say and what users do?
